event: An angel sounding the trumpet of the Last Judgement, rendered in abstract expressionist form
symbols: explosive color and movement — cosmic upheaval at the end of days
trumpet-like forms — the angelic trumpet call announcing judgement
red and fiery tones — divine wrath and purifying fire
fragmented forms — dissolution of the present world order
black sweeping lines — dramatic division between salvation and condemnation
brilliant yellows and oranges — divine radiance breaking through chaos
description: An intensely dynamic abstract painting dominated by explosive swirls of vivid color—deep reds, fiery oranges, brilliant yellows, and patches of green and blue. Bold black curving lines sweep diagonally across the canvas, creating a sense of violent motion and cosmic energy. Amid the abstraction, suggestions of a winged figure or trumpet-blowing form can be discerned in the upper portion, though the composition resists literal representation. The brushwork is vigorous and gestural, with thick impasto and areas of translucent layering.
theological_description: Kandinsky's abstract interpretation of an angel of the Last Judgement strips the apocalyptic vision to its emotional and spiritual essence. Rather than depicting the event literally, the painting conveys the overwhelming, terrifying, and awe-inspiring nature of divine judgement through pure visual force. In Revelation, the sounding of angelic trumpets initiates successive waves of cosmic catastrophe and divine intervention (Revelation 8-11). The seventh trumpet announces that 'the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ' (Revelation 11:15). Jesus himself taught that at His return, angels would be sent 'with a loud trumpet call' to gather the elect from the four winds (Matthew 24:31). The abstract treatment is theologically apt: the Last Judgement exceeds human capacity for representation, as Paul writes, 'no eye has seen, nor ear heard, nor the heart of man imagined, what God has prepared' (1 Corinthians 2:9). Kandinsky, who wrote extensively on the spiritual dimension of art in 'Concerning the Spiritual in Art' (1911), saw abstraction as a means of accessing inner spiritual realities that figurative art could not reach. The chaotic energy of the painting captures the 'great and awesome day of the Lord' (Joel 2:31) where the present order is shattered and remade by divine power.

scripture_refs.reason: Seven angels given seven trumpets to sound at the Last Judgement
The seventh trumpet announces the consummation of God's kingdom
Angels sent with a great trumpet to gather the elect at Christ's return
At the last trumpet the dead will be raised imperishable
The abstract form reflects the ineffability of eschatological realities beyond human imagination
The great white throne judgement where the dead are judged
